*Zh panel \(d\) is from \(n=1\) huehuetenangensis (+8 perennis); poorly sampled.
Three things the reads confirm:

The REF-background floor is \(\varepsilon \approx 0.004\)–\(0.007\), exactly the per-read error rate — the REF state emits at the noise floor, not 0 (matching the nilhifi anchoring rationale).
The read-based \(d\) reproduces the panel-genotype \(d\) for the four well-sampled donors: the interval \([\,2\times\text{HET},\ \text{HOM}\,]\) brackets \(d\), and both land at \(\approx 0.3\)–\(0.4\) against the panel’s \(0.28\)–\(0.35\) (Zl essentially exact: 0.329 vs 0.333). Two fully independent measurements — donor-panel genotype composition and NIL read fractions — agree that d ≈ 0.3, not ≈ 1. The reads run slightly high, consistent with

the specific donor accessions carrying more alt than the species average and
HET segments absorbing some true-HOM markers (see next point).

The HET/HOM split is not clean — as predicted. Under the model \(2\times\)HET should equal HOM; instead HOM \(>2\times\)HET (e.g. Zx 0.49 vs 0.37) because at \(0.4\times\) the caller thresholds the alt-fraction rather than resolving zygosity, so HET keeps the low tail and HOM the high tail. The estimator’s own ambiguity is the identifiability limit this document is about. Zh is the extreme case (HET alt-frac 0.34, HOM 0.89): huehuetenangensis is the most divergent donor, so \(d\) is genuinely highest, but the value is uncertain (panel \(n=1\), strong het/hom bleed).

Estimates: agent/dose_estimates_by_taxon.csv.


2.6 The site-selection ceiling
Restricting to sites where the donor is fixed-ALT (\(f_2\to1\), \(d\to1\)) would restore the clean \(\{0, \tfrac12, 1\}\) model. But “fixed in this donor” cannot be known without the donor accession’s own genotypes, which we do not have; the --min-af 0.05:minor species panel identifies common variants, not accession-fixed ones. This is a more basic reference-data gap than phasing/LD: without the donor genotype we cannot even label which markers are informative for a given cross. It is the fundamental reason low-coverage genotype-imputation results from human aDNA or inbred-founder PHGs do not transfer here.

title: Why the SNP50K skim cannot resolve zygosity — two mechanisms, with the math
text:
3. Consequences

Fit the emission means, don’t assume them. The state means are \(\approx\{0,\,d/2,\,d\}\) with a per-donor \(d\); a fixed \(\{ \varepsilon, \tfrac12,
1-\varepsilon\}\) emission is mis-specified. This is why RTIGER’s per-taxon fit_means outperforms the fixed-mean count emission on this data.
Prefer donor-enriched sites where possible. Selecting sites with high species-level \(f_2\) raises \(d\) toward 1 and widens state separation, up to the accession ceiling in §2.6.
Report honestly. The donor footprint (where introgression is) is recoverable; the het-vs-donor-hom split is marginal (\(0.15\) vs \(0.30\)); per-site zygosity is not recoverable and should not be claimed. For B1/anthocyanin QTL the mapping unit is the segment/dosage genotype, obtained by projecting the decoded ancestry state onto the markers within a segment — not per-marker calls.

title: Why the SNP50K skim cannot resolve zygosity — two mechanisms, with the math
text:
Reproduce
D=/Volumes/rsstu/users/r/rrellan/BZea/bzeaseq/50K/results/joint

# §1.4 cohort alt-allele frequency spectrum (justifies p small)
bcftools query -f '%AC\t%AN\n' "$D/cohort.vcf.gz" \
 | awk '$1!="."&&$2>0{p=$1/$2;n++;s+=p;if(p<.01)b++} END{print "mean AF",s/n,"  frac<0.01",b/n}'

# §2.3 per-species donor dose d = f2 + 0.5*f1  (ref_<species>.txt = panel sample lists)
bcftools view -S ref_Z__parviglumis.txt --force-samples "$D/bzea_50K_ref_panel.vcf.gz" \
 | bcftools query -f '[%GT\n]' | awk -F'[/|]' '
   {n++; if($1=="."||$2==".")next; if($1==0&&$2==0)r++; else if($1==$2)a++; else h++}
   END{c=r+h+a; printf "REF %.3f HET %.3f ALT %.3f  d=%.3f\n",r/c,h/c,a/c,(a+0.5*h)/c}'

title: Why the SNP50K skim cannot resolve zygosity — two mechanisms, with the math
text:
References

Grzybowski MW, Mural RV, Xu G, Turkus J, Yang J, Schnable JC (2023). A common resequencing-based genetic marker data set for global maize diversity. The Plant Journal 113(6):1109–1121. doi:10.1111/tpj.16123. — the teosinte reference panel used to estimate the donor dose (§2).
Li H (2011). A statistical framework for SNP calling, mutation discovery, association mapping and population genetical parameter estimation from sequencing data. Bioinformatics 27(21):2987–2993. doi:10.1093/bioinformatics/btr509. — the samtools/bcftools caller and GL model behind the call -mv prior (§1).
Hui R, D’Atanasio E, Cassidy LM, Scheib CL, Kivisild T (2020). Evaluating genotype imputation pipeline for ultra-low coverage ancient genomes. Scientific Reports 10:18542. doi:10.1038/s41598-020-75387-w. — reference-panel (aDNA) imputation that does not transfer here (§2.6).
Jensen SE, Charles JR, Muleta K, et al. (2020). A sorghum practical haplotype graph facilitates genome-wide imputation and cost-effective genomic prediction. The Plant Genome 13(1):e20009. doi:10.1002/tpg2.20009. — inbred-founder PHG imputation that does not transfer here (§2.6).

Full caller / genotype-likelihood method-paper references (GATK HaplotypeCaller, ANGSD, Li–Stephens) are in the companion genotype_likelihoods_and_hmm.html.

Analysis 2026-07-02, cohort.vcf.gz / bzea_50K_ref_panel.vcf.gz (read-only mount). Companion to snp50k-cohort-provenance.md. Panel: Grzybowski et al. 2023, --min-af 0.05:minor, not imputed. ``````````````````` :::

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
text:
Scope. This note documents and defends an assumption used by the wideseq benchmark generator (make_wideseq_benchmark.R): that the per-sample coverage distribution can stand in for the per-site coverage distribution when simulating the ~27.6M teosinte-vs-B73 wideseq sites. The motivation is practical — per-site statistics over ~1,400 samples × ~27.6M sites is an unwieldy (sample × site) tensor, whereas per-sample and per-bin summaries are tractable. The point of this note is to state what is actually assumed, assess where it holds and where it does not, and lay out how to defend it (and partly replace it with measurement) for reviewers.
Framing — ergodic inspiration, scale-invariance claim. The inspiration is ergodic: the two axes here — the ensemble of samples and the sequence of positions (1 Mb bins) along the chromosomes — play the roles of ensemble and “time,” and ergodicity’s promise is that averaging along one genome equals averaging across the ensemble. That symmetry is what motivates re-using a per-sample coverage law at the per-bin scale. But the genomic axis is non-stationary (pericentromeres/repeats are systematically low), so the process is not ergodic in the textbook sense. The rigorous, testable claim we actually rely on is therefore narrower: that the missingness law missing(λ) is scale-invariant — the same function whether λ varies because you picked a low-depth sample or a low-mappability region — i.e. λ is a near-sufficient statistic for missingness. Ergodicity is the analogy; scale-invariance of missing(λ) is the claim.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
text:
1. What is actually being assumed
The slogan “per-sample coverage ≈ per-site coverage” is, taken literally, false and should not be defended as written. The defensible claim is narrower and has two parts.

Within-sample exchangeability of sites within a bin. Every wideseq site in a sample is modeled as accumulating reads at a common per-sample rate λ_s, with the only site-level variation being Poisson read sampling plus a structural “always-missing” floor π. There is no systematic per-site coverage structure (GC, mappability, local copy number) beyond that floor.
Transfer of the between-sample law to the within-sample / per-bin scale. The missingness curve and the coverage distribution are estimated across samples, then applied across bins within a sample: a low-coverage bin is assumed to behave like a low-coverage sample.

Named precisely, this is scale-invariance of the missingness law: missing(λ) estimated across samples is the same function as missing(λ) across bins within a genome (equivalently, λ is a near-sufficient statistic for missingness at both scales). That is the testable core the ergodic symmetry points to — and section 4 tests it.

What the generator does

Per-sample mean coverage is drawn λ_s ~ Normal(0.590, 0.226), floored for positivity. (Fit to 1,434 BZea wideseq samples; Normal beats Gamma and lognormal by AIC −192 vs +205 vs +1091; CV 0.38, skew 0.34.)
Missingness uses the wideseq exp-floor model missing(λ) = π + (1−π)·e^(−kλ) with π = 0.346, k = 1.256 (mean missingness ≈ 0.67), fit across samples (missing_data.Rmd).
Each 1 Mb bin then draws, analytically and without materializing individual variants: VARIANT_COUNT ~ Poisson(sites_per_mb), INFORMATIVE_VARIANT_COUNT ~ Binomial(VARIANT_COUNT, present_prob), DEPTH_SUM = IVC + Poisson(IVC·(λ/present−1)), ALT_COUNT ~ Binomial(DEPTH_SUM, p_eff).

So λ_s and the population (π, k) are the only coverage inputs; every bin in a sample shares them. That sharing is the scale-invariance assumption — the ergodic symmetry — in operational form.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
section: 2. Assessment: where it is safe, where it bites
text:
2. Assessment: where it is safe, where it bites

Safe — and this is the load-bearing argument
The caller never sees a site. It consumes the per-bin ALT_FREQ, an aggregate over ~3,000–4,000 informative sites (real per-bin means: DEPTH_SUM ≈ 7,600 reads, INFORMATIVE_VARIANT_COUNT ≈ 4,300, ≈ 1.8 reads per covered site — shallow coverage, many sites). By the central limit theorem the bin statistic is governed by the mean per-site rate (≈ λ_s) and the effective site count, not by the shape of the per-site coverage distribution:
Var(ALT_FREQ) ≈ p(1 − p) / DEPTH_SUM
Consequently the per-site coverage decomposition is a nuisance the caller cannot distinguish and does not need: matching the per-bin DEPTH_SUM and informative-site count is sufficient to match calling behavior. The assumption is strongest exactly where it is used — at the bin aggregation scale.


Where it can genuinely bite
None of these is the per-sample-mean substitution itself; they are the real targets a careful reviewer will probe.

Spatial coverage structure (the principal risk). If low-coverage / low-mappability sites cluster — maize pericentromeres, repeats — then bins are not exchangeable: heterochromatic bins carry systematically fewer informative sites and lower depth than a flat sites_per_mb + constant π implies. This understates bin-to-bin variance and over-covers the hardest regions.
Reference-mapping bias against donor alleles. Teosinte (ALT) reads map worse to the B73 reference, so donor sites can be under-covered and under-called — a coverage × genotype interaction that a genotype-independent λ_s ignores. This is the same effect that makes the 0.4× skim under-call donor, and it is what a reviewer focused on introgression calling will attack first.
Between → within transfer of (π, k). The parameters are fit from between-sample variation but applied across bins within a sample. This is valid only if the missingness mechanism is the same at both scales.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
section: 3. How to defend it — the cheap data is the key
text:
3. How to defend it — the cheap data is the key
The same scale that makes the per-site tensor unwieldy (1,400 × 27.6M) is why it is not needed: the per-bin file (all_samples_bin_genotypes.tsv, ~3M rows) is exactly the granularity at which the assumption can be tested rather than asserted. In rough order of impact:

Reframe the claim as bin-level sufficiency, not coverage equivalence. State that the inferential unit is the 1 Mb bin; the caller is a function of ALT_FREQ and the informative-site count; per-site coverage enters only through those bin aggregates; therefore matching the per-bin DEPTH_SUM / INFORMATIVE_VARIANT_COUNT distributions is sufficient. This preempts the “per-site ≠ per-sample” objection at the root.
Run the direct ergodicity test (cheap, decisive). From the per-bin file, fit missing ~ π + (1−π)e^(−kλ) within samples across bins and overlay it on the between-sample (π, k). If the within-sample bin-level curve coincides with the between-sample curve, the ergodic transfer is empirically validated — one figure answers the objection.
Validate the aggregate, not the atoms. Overlay the simulated per-bin DEPTH_SUM and ALT_FREQ distributions on the real per-bin distributions. Coincidence justifies the collapse at the scale that matters, irrespective of per-site detail.
Where it is cheap, replace the assumption with measurement. Instead of flat sites_per_mb + constant π, feed the real per-bin marginal profile (mean informative-site count and present-fraction per genomic bin, averaged over samples — directly computable from the per-bin file) into the generator. This neutralizes the spatial-structure risk and leaves ergodicity responsible only for sub-bin site behavior, which the CLT already protects. The weakest assumption becomes an empirical input.
Pre-empt with a sensitivity analysis. Simulate per-site rate heterogeneity (e.g. site rates ~ Gamma with the same per-sample mean) and show the bin-level ALT_FREQ distribution and the caller’s accuracy are invariant. “We injected per-site coverage dispersion and bin-level inference did not move” is a refutation, not a promise.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
section: 4. Validation result (move 2, run on the real data)
text:
4. Validation result (move 2, run on the real data)
We ran move 2 on the real per-bin file (all_samples_bin_genotypes.tsv, 1,434 samples, 3.06M bins; agent/ergodicity_check.R / agent/ergodicity_real_vs_sim.R): fit missing = π + (1−π)e^(−kλ) within genomes across bins and between genomes across samples, and compare. As a control we ran the identical fit on the simulation.



dataset
scale
n
π
k




real
within-genome (per 1 Mb bin)
3,057,288
0.313
1.176

between-genome (per sample)
1,434

within-genome (bin)
212,900

between-genome (sample)
100
0.346
1.256






Within- vs between-genome missingness, real vs simulation


Read this carefully — the test is only meaningful on real data. The simulation is ergodic by construction: make_*_benchmark.R draws every bin from one per-sample λ and a flat per-bin present_prob, so its within- and between-genome curves are identical to 3 decimals (Δπ ≈ 0, Δk ≈ 0.001) and recover the input (π=0.346, k=1.256) exactly. That is the control, not the validation — a coincidence on simulated data would prove nothing.
The real data is what validates the assumption: its within- and between-genome curves nearly coincide too (Δπ = 0.033, Δk = 0.080), separating only slightly in the high-λ tail (> 1×). So the missingness↔︎coverage law measured across samples does hold across bins within a genome — the per-sample coverage model transfers to the per-bin scale. The small residual (the real data being slightly less ergodic than the perfect simulation: bin floor 0.313 vs sample 0.346) is exactly the genuine per-bin spatial structure flagged below — now quantified and shown to be small.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
text:
5. Limitations to state honestly

Spatial coverage structure (heterochromatin / repeats) — the source of the small real-data ergodicity residual above (Δπ ≈ 0.03); mitigated by move 4 (real per-bin profile); otherwise a flat per-Mb density understates bin-to-bin variance.
Reference bias on donor alleles — a separate coverage × genotype term, checkable by asking whether ALT_FREQ reaches ~1.0 in known homozygous-donor regions of high-confidence samples; if it falls short, add an ALT-specific coverage/error term.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
text:
6. Bottom line
The assumption is defensible, provided the narrow version is defended: bin-level sufficiency with within-bin site exchangeability, protected by the CLT at ~10³–10⁴ sites per bin — not the literal “per-sample equals per-site coverage.” The per-bin file lets three of the objections become validation figures and one become a direct empirical input, so ergodicity can be presented as tested, and partly replaced by measurement, rather than assumed. The highest-value check — move 2, the within- vs between-genome missingness-curve overlay — has now been run on the real data and confirms the transfer (Δπ = 0.03, Δk = 0.08; section 4), with the simulation control showing the expected Δ ≈ 0.

title: The ergodicity assumption in the wideseq coverage model
text:
References / artifacts

Generator: make_wideseq_benchmark.R (per-bin analytic draw; λ ~ Normal).
Coverage / missingness model: BzeaSeq docs/missing_data.Rmd (λ = DEPTH_SUM/VARIANT_COUNT; exp-floor fit), wideseq row λ=0.59, π=0.346, k=1.256.
Per-sample λ fit: Normal(0.590, 0.226), n=1,434 (agent/fit_wideseq_lambda.R).
Per-bin data for validation: …/BZea/bzeaseq/ancestry/all_samples_bin_genotypes.tsv.
Caller scored against this benchmark: score_wideseq.R (3-state Kgmm_HMM).

title: Per-coverage caller calibration on simulated TeoNAM truth
text:
What this is. The coverage-degradation GWAS sweep is only fair if each ancestry caller runs at its per-coverage optimal parameter. This note is the calibration that produces those parameters: for each caller and each coverage \(\lambda\) we simulate a known-truth NIL panel, degrade it to \(\lambda\) with the same read model the sweep uses, sweep the caller’s one segmentation parameter, and pick the value that minimizes the donor-fragment false-discovery rate.






NoteMethod (identical across callers)




Simulated ground truth — simulate_nil("BC1S4", n = 200, map = <118K v5 cM grid>, seed = 12345), with known crossovers. Truth is simulated, never a real family’s caller output (that would score a caller against another caller).
Degrade with the sweep’s read model — .draw_counts(state, λ, error = 0.01), same seeds — at \(\lambda \in \{0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, \infty\}\) (\(\infty\) = deterministic depth-100).
Sweep the caller’s single parameter and score each fit against truth.
Pick by minimum donor_frag_FDR (fewest spurious donor fragments), at min_reads = 1. Donor recall stays \(\approx 0.99\)–\(1.0\) throughout, so recall does not discriminate — FDR does.



The three callers differ only in the segmentation parameter and whether it is map-aware:









caller
parameter
type
map-aware?




RTIGER
rigidity
integer — minimum run length in markers
no (index-based)


nNIL
rrate
continuous — per-marker recombination rate (self-stay \(= 1-\)rrate)
no (one fixed transition every marker)


LB-Impute
recombdist
continuous — linkage-decay distance (cM)
yes (consumes the cM gaps)





Show code
suppressMessages({
  library(here)
  library(data.table)
  library(ggplot2)
})

COVS <- c("0.1", "0.2", "0.5", "1", "5", "10", "20", "Inf")
read_pick <- function(caller) {
  d <- fread(here(sprintf("results/sim/teonam/%s_calib_bycov.csv", caller)))
  setnames(d, tolower(names(d)))
  parm <- intersect(c("rigidity", "rrate", "recombdist"), names(d))
  data.table(
    caller = caller, coverage = as.character(d$coverage),
    param = parm, param_value = d[[parm]],
    fdr = d[["fdr"]], recall = d[["recall"]], dice = d[["dice"]]
  )
}
picks <- rbindlist(lapply(c("rtiger", "nnil", "lbimpute"), read_pick))
picks[, coverage := factor(coverage, levels = COVS)]
picks[, caller := factor(caller, levels = c("rtiger", "nnil", "lbimpute"))]

curves <- rbindlist(lapply(c("rtiger", "nnil", "lbimpute"), function(c) {
  d <- fread(here(sprintf("results/sim/teonam/%s_calib_bycov_sweep.csv", c)))
  d[, caller := c]
  d
}), fill = TRUE)
curves[, coverage := factor(as.character(coverage), levels = COVS)]
curves[, caller := factor(caller, levels = c("rtiger", "nnil", "lbimpute"))]



The chosen parameter by coverage
param*(λ), the value carried into the sweep at each coverage. Each caller’s parameter lives on a different scale, so the panels have independent \(y\)-axes.


Show code
knitr::kable(
  dcast(picks, coverage ~ caller, value.var = "param_value"),
  caption = "param*(λ): rigidity (RTIGER), rrate (nNIL), recombdist cM (LB-Impute)."
)



param*(λ): rigidity (RTIGER), rrate (nNIL), recombdist cM (LB-Impute).

rtiger
nnil
lbimpute

Inf

0.0000171
8.224







Show code
ggplot(picks, aes(coverage, param_value, group = caller, colour = caller)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  facet_wrap(~caller, scales = "free_y",
    labeller = as_labeller(c(
      rtiger = "RTIGER · rigidity (markers)",
      nnil = "nNIL · rrate",
      lbimpute = "LB-Impute · recombdist (cM)"
    ))
  ) +
  labs(x = "coverage λ (×)", y = "chosen parameter", colour = NULL) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")










RTIGER rigidity rises with coverage (16 → 128): deeper coverage exposes more per-marker detail, so a longer minimum run is needed to keep segments stable.
LB-Impute recombdist is high at the lowest coverages (stiffer, longer segments compensate for sparse reads) and settles to \(\approx 8\)–25 cM once coverage is informative.
nNIL rrate does not trend — it bounces over an order of magnitude. That is not signal; see the flat FDR plateau below.



Why each pick — FDR against the parameter
The selection objective, donor_frag_FDR, as a function of the swept parameter, one line per coverage. The chosen parameter is the curve’s minimum.


Show code
# Mark each pick at its own recorded (param*, FDR*) — robust to golden-refined
# values that need not sit on the coarse swept grid.
mark <- picks[, .(caller, coverage, value = param_value, donor_frag_FDR = fdr)]

ggplot(curves, aes(value, donor_frag_FDR, colour = coverage, group = coverage)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(data = mark, size = 2.4, shape = 21, fill = "white", stroke = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~caller, ncol = 1, scales = "free_x",
    labeller = as_labeller(c(
      rtiger = "RTIGER · rigidity", nnil = "nNIL · rrate",
      lbimpute = "LB-Impute · recombdist (cM)"
    ))
  ) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(
    x = "swept parameter (log scale)", y = "donor-fragment FDR",
    colour = "λ (×)",
    title = "Selection curves — chosen value (○) minimizes donor-fragment FDR"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12)









nNIL is a flat low-rrate plateau. Across the whole low-rrate range the FDR barely moves (recall is already \(\approx 1\)), so the “minimum” is essentially noise — which is why the per-coverage rrate* bounces around above. The operational decision is therefore to run nNIL at a single fixed rrate = 3.3e-5 in the sweep rather than the per-coverage argmin; RTIGER and LB-Impute do carry their per-coverage values.


Recall is not the discriminating metric
Donor-marker recall is saturated (\(\approx 0.99\)–\(1.0\)) at every coverage and parameter, confirming that donor_frag_FDR — not recall — is what separates a good parameter from a bad one.


Show code
ggplot(picks, aes(coverage, recall, group = caller, colour = caller)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0.98, 1)) +
  labs(x = "coverage λ (×)", y = "donor-marker recall at param*", colour = NULL) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")











Bottom line
The calibration gives each caller a coverage-matched parameter for the degradation sweep: RTIGER’s rigidity climbs 16 → 128 with depth, LB-Impute’s recombdist settles near 8–25 cM, and nNIL’s FDR plateau means a single fixed rrate is the honest choice. All three hold donor recall at \(\approx 1\); the tuning only trades off spurious donor fragments (FDR). Parameters are read from results/sim/teonam/{rtiger,nnil,lbimpute}_calib_bycov.csv; the full selection curves are in the _calib_bycov_sweep.csv companions.

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
Does the Broman/simcross BC2S3 simulation reproduce the single-locus breeding-scheme expectation for the REF/Het/ALT genome fractions? This notebook runs that check on the TeoNAM native v5 cM map (the same 118K-marker grid the per-coverage caller calibration uses), validating the simulator this repo’s coverage-degradation sweeps rely on. It is a circularity check: before trusting a caller’s recovery of the truth, confirm the truth-generator itself is unbiased.
The sim stack is zealhmm’s own (R/simulate.R: .bcsft_pedigree, .simulate_dosage); the single-locus expectation and the design-correct goodness-of-fit test are in R/single_locus.R (ported from the exploratory zealtiger repo). Why Hotelling and not Pearson, why Mb on real marker coordinates, and the ergodicity distinction are explained inline.

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
Setup


Show code
suppressMessages({
  library(data.table)
  library(dplyr)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(simcross)
  library(patchwork)
  library(here)
})
source(here("R/simulate.R")) # .bcsft_pedigree, .chr_cm_lengths, .simulate_dosage
source(here("R/single_locus.R")) # single_locus_p0, state_fractions_mb, hotelling_fractions, plot_fraction_forest
set.seed(params$seed)

# TeoNAM native v5 cM map — the 118K-marker grid used by the caller calibration.
mc <- fread(here("data/teonam/markers_v5_gwas118k_cm.tsv"))
map <- mc[, .(chr = as.integer(chr), bp = as.integer(pos_v5), cm = as.numeric(cm))][order(chr, bp)]
markers <- map # the native markers directly carry chr/bp/cm — no re-gridding

bp <- .bcsft_pedigree(n_bc = 2L, n_self = 3L)
ped <- bp$ped
nid <- bp$nil_id
cmlen <- .chr_cm_lengths(map)
cat(sprintf(
  "simcross %s | native markers: %d | genome: %.0f cM | N: %d\n",
  as.character(packageVersion("simcross")), nrow(markers), sum(cmlen$L), params$n
))


simcross 0.8 | native markers: 118514 | genome: 1559 cM | N: 1500

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
1. The single-locus expectation (BC2S3 transition matrix)
The genotype distribution at any single locus after F1 → 2 backcrosses → 3 selfings, from the breeding-scheme transition matrices (map- and interference-independent — a marginal quantity).


Show code
p0 <- single_locus_p0(n_bc = 2L, n_self = 3L)
tibble(state = names(p0), expected = as.numeric(p0)) |>
  mutate(note = c("hom recurrent", "het", "hom donor")) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 6)

  p0["ALT"] + 0.5 * p0["Het"]

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
2. f_i validation (Mb-based, Hotelling)
Simulate n NILs on the native map, tabulate per-NIL Mb fractions, and test the population means against p0 with the design-correct Hotelling/Wald χ²(2) (sample = unit; empirical between-sample covariance — not per-marker binomial, which linkage overdispersion would make reject a correct sampler).


Show code
F <- t(vapply(seq_len(params$n), function(i) {
  state_fractions_mb(markers, .simulate_dosage(ped, cmlen, markers,
    m = params$m, p = params$p, nil_id = nid
  ))
}, numeric(3)))
colnames(F) <- c("REF", "Het", "ALT")
res <- hotelling_fractions(F, p0)
knitr::kable(res$table, digits = 5)

  res$T2, res$p_chisq,
  with(res$table[res$table$state == "ALT", ], expected * (1 - expected) / sd_fi^2),
  nrow(markers)

Hotelling T^2 = 0.474 (df=2), chi2 p = 0.7891 | N_eff(ALT) = 23.1 vs 118514 markers


Show code
cat(if (all(res$table$in_ci)) {
  "PASS: all expectations inside 95% Wald CIs.\n"
} else {
  "CHECK: an expectation outside its CI.\n"
})


PASS: all expectations inside 95% Wald CIs.


The fractions span ~2 orders of magnitude, so a single linear forest plot can show magnitude or intervals but not both. Panel A (log axis) carries magnitude; panel B (standardized deviation, ±1.96 band) carries the pass criterion — scale-free.


Show code
plot_fraction_forest(res$table,
  title = sprintf(
    "BC2S3 vs single-locus expectation (n=%d, native v5 map)",
    params$n
  )
)









N_eff (not the marker count) is the ergodicity-limited effective sample size: with ~33 crossovers/genome the per-NIL fraction averages over few independent blocks, so the right SE is sd(f_i)/√N, not a per-marker binomial. This is the same ergodicity distinction developed in the wideseq coverage-ergodicity note.

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
3. Marker-resolution sweep (deferred)






NoteDeferred to a native-map re-run



The exploratory version swept marker density (Cohen’s d of the Het fraction vs spacing) to show Het is undercounted only below ~0.5 Mb spacing — a truth-measurement floor distinct from low-coverage Het miscalling. That sweep was computed on the maize consensus map; reproducing it on the TeoNAM native grid needs a density-varying marker grid (zealhmm’s build_marker_grid currently integer-overflows on bp-scale maize spans — a small fix to make before running). Left as future work; the layer-1 truth validation above (§1–§2) does not depend on it.

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
Conclusion
On the TeoNAM native v5 map, the simcross BC2S3 simulator reproduces the single-locus genotype fractions — all three states inside their 95% CIs, donor dosage 0.125 — so the truth-generator behind the coverage-degradation sweeps is unbiased at the single-locus margin. The only known deviation is a marker-resolution undercount of Het at coarse spacing (§3, negligible below ~0.5 Mb). Layer 1 (truth) is validated; caller observation/recovery under coverage is the calibration and sweep notebooks.

title: Local Ancestry Projection, GWAS Signal Geometry, and the Sources of Association Dispersion
text:
Genome-wide association studies performed in experimental mapping populations differ fundamentally from GWAS performed in natural populations.
The allele distribution in a mapping population is not determined by Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium. Instead, it is determined by the breeding design, founder genotypes, segregation scheme, and recombination history.
Therefore, classical population-genetic approximations based on
\[
2p(1-p)
\]
are not generally appropriate.
The expected GWAS signal should instead be described in terms of the variance of the actual genetic predictor segregating in the mapping population.






NoteEmpirical motivation — two results in this repo



This framework was prompted by two observations in the TeoNAM STAM coverage-degradation notebook:

the interpolated-grid step collapses the GWAS significance dispersion onto an almost-single profile — an artifact of neighbour interpolation rather than a biological signal; and
the RTIGER coverage-degradation sweep shows that introducing uncertainty through a subpar ancestry method (neighbour interpolation) inflates the het fraction and the significance-profile noise.

The framework below formalizes what those results show empirically: the spatial geometry of the GWAS profile is itself a diagnostic of ancestry-inference quality.

title: Evaluating Local Ancestry HMMs Through the Geometry of the GWAS Association Landscape
text:
Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) performed using low-coverage sequencing data are affected by uncertainty arising from sequencing errors, genotype calling errors, and imperfect imputation. Traditionally, Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) for local ancestry inference are evaluated using metrics such as ancestry state accuracy, switch error, or breakpoint localization. While these measures assess the quality of ancestry reconstruction, they do not directly evaluate the downstream objective for which the inferred ancestry is ultimately used.
This work proposes evaluating HMMs from a different perspective: by examining the geometry of the resulting GWAS association landscape.
The central hypothesis is that a more accurate reconstruction of the latent ancestry process should produce a GWAS profile that is spatially more coherent, less noisy, and therefore possesses greater statistical power.






NoteEmpirical motivation — two results in this repo



This framework was prompted by two observations in the TeoNAM STAM coverage-degradation notebook:

the interpolated-grid step collapses the GWAS significance dispersion onto an almost-single profile — an artifact of neighbour interpolation rather than a biological signal; and
the RTIGER coverage-degradation sweep shows that introducing uncertainty through a subpar ancestry method (neighbour interpolation) inflates the het fraction and the significance-profile noise.

The measures below formalize what those results show empirically: the spatial geometry of the GWAS profile is itself a diagnostic of ancestry-inference quality.

title: Local Ancestry Projection, GWAS Signal Geometry, and the Sources of Association Dispersion
text:
Wavelet Detail Energy
Wavelet decompositions separate local high-frequency fluctuations from large-scale structure. High detail energy indicates local roughness and therefore increased noise.

title: Local Ancestry Projection, GWAS Signal Geometry, and the Sources of Association Dispersion
text:
Fourier High-Frequency Energy
Because random fluctuations introduce rapid oscillations, noise increases the proportion of spectral power contained in high frequencies. The normalized quantity
\[
\frac{\sum_{f>f_c}P(f)}
{\sum_fP(f)}
\]
provides one measure of roughness (a band-limited version of it — power between two physical cutoffs — is what the caller-noisiness table computes).

title: Evaluating Local Ancestry HMMs Through the Geometry of the GWAS Association Landscape
text:
Spatial Autocorrelation
The most intuitive measure is spatial autocorrelation,
\[
\rho(d)
=
\operatorname{cor}(S(x),S(x+d)),
\]
where \(d\) denotes genomic distance.
If neighboring markers exhibit similar association evidence,
autocorrelation remains high.
If marker-level errors dominate,
autocorrelation rapidly decays.
Unlike spectral measures, autocorrelation possesses an immediate biological interpretation.
A lag-one autocorrelation near one implies that neighboring markers carry nearly identical evidence.
A lag-one autocorrelation near zero indicates that neighboring markers behave almost independently.

title: Local Ancestry Projection, GWAS Signal Geometry, and the Sources of Association Dispersion
text:
Autocorrelation summaries
The lag-one autocorrelation
\[
\rho_1
=
\operatorname{cor}(S_i,S_{i+1}),
\]
and the integrated autocorrelation
\[
Q
=
\sum_{k=1}^{K}\rho(k)
\]
summarize coherence in the lag domain. A lag-one autocorrelation near one implies neighboring markers carry nearly identical evidence; near zero, they behave almost independently. Models producing larger \(Q\) generate more coherent landscapes.

title: Single-locus validation of the BC2S3 simulation
text:
3. Marker-resolution sweep
The fractions above are measured on the full native grid. How does the Het fraction degrade as the grid coarsens? Short Het tracts that fall below the marker spacing are missed — a truth-measurement floor, distinct from the low-coverage Het miscalling an ancestry caller faces (layer 2). We simulate one set of NILs once and re-measure each on grids of increasing density (built by build_marker_grid), reporting Cohen’s \(d=(\text{mean}-\text{expected})/\text{SD}\) of the Het fraction — an N-independent effect size, negative when Het is undercounted.


Show code
set.seed(params$seed + 1L)
n_sweep <- min(params$n, 800L)
# simulate the shared meiosis once per NIL, then read dosage on any grid
sims <- lapply(seq_len(n_sweep), function(i) {
  simcross::sim_from_pedigree(ped, L = cmlen$L, m = params$m, p = params$p)
})
dosage_from_sim <- function(sim, mk) {
  d <- integer(nrow(mk))
  for (ci in seq_len(nrow(cmlen))) {
    rows <- which(mk$chr == cmlen$chr[ci])
    if (!length(rows)) next
    nil <- sim[[ci]][[nid]]
    cm <- mk$cm[rows]
    d[rows] <- (.hap_allele_at(nil$mat, cm) == 2L) + (.hap_allele_at(nil$pat, cm) == 2L)
  }
  d
}
dens <- c(500L, 1000L, 2000L, 4000L, 8000L, 16000L)
sw <- rbindlist(lapply(dens, function(D) {
  mk <- build_marker_grid(map, n_markers = D)
  het <- vapply(sims, function(s) state_fractions_mb(mk, dosage_from_sim(s, mk))[["Het"]], numeric(1))
  gap <- mk[, as.double(stats::median(diff(sort(bp)))), by = chr][, stats::median(V1)]
  data.table(
    markers = nrow(mk), spacing_Mb = round(gap / 1e6, 3),
    Het_mean = round(mean(het), 4),
    Het_d = round((mean(het) - p0[["Het"]]) / stats::sd(het), 3)
  )
}))
knitr::kable(sw, caption = sprintf(
  "Het fraction vs marker density (same %d NILs re-measured on each grid; expected Het = %.4f). Cohen's d = (mean - expected)/SD.",
  n_sweep, p0[["Het"]]
))



Het fraction vs marker density (same 800 NILs re-measured on each grid; expected Het = 0.0312). Cohen’s d = (mean - expected)/SD.

Show code
ggplot(sw, aes(spacing_Mb, Het_d)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = -Inf, xmax = Inf, ymin = -0.05, ymax = 0.05, fill = "grey90") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2c7fb8") +
  geom_point(colour = "#2c7fb8", size = 2) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(
    x = "marker spacing (Mb, log)", y = "Het Cohen's d = (mean − expected)/SD",
    title = "Het is undercounted at coarse marker spacing",
    subtitle = "grey band = |d| < 0.05 (negligible)"
  ) +
  theme_bw()









Reading it: Het is undercounted (\(d<0\)) at coarse spacing — small Het segments fall below the marker resolution and are lost — and relaxes to a negligible residual (\(|d|<0.05\)) as the grid densifies. This is a truth-measurement floor, not a caller artifact; the layer-1 validation (§1–§2) does not depend on it.

title: Local Ancestry Projection, GWAS Signal Geometry, and the Sources of Association Dispersion
text:
A three-axis test
For each parameter set \(\theta\), measure three independent quantities:

Ancestry-reconstruction accuracy against simulated truth, \(E_A(\theta)=P(\hat A_\theta\neq A_{\mathrm{true}})\) — state accuracy, donor recall, false-switch rate, breakpoint error, tract-length distribution.
GWAS-landscape coherence, \(\rho(d)\) or the band-limited FFT noise \(N_{\mathrm{FFT}}=\sum_{f>f_c}P(f)/\sum_f P(f)\) — the caller-noisiness table’s acf_lag1 / fft_band.
Biological mapping performance \(M(\theta)\): recovery of simulated QTL, peak-localization error, effect-size recovery, false-positive rate.

title: Local Ancestry Projection, GWAS Signal Geometry, and the Sources of Association Dispersion
text:
What discriminates the hypotheses

Under H1: \(\arg\max_\theta \rho(\theta)\neq\arg\min_\theta E_A(\theta)\) — the smoothest profile is not the most accurate reconstruction, and coherence keeps rising with more smoothing.
Under H2: \(\arg\min_\theta E_A(\theta)\approx\arg\max_\theta M(\theta)\) — accuracy and mapping performance peak together, at the resolution matching the true ancestry-block scale; beyond it, over-smoothing loses breakpoints and mapping precision falls.

So the correct optimization target is not \(\max_\theta \rho(\theta)\) (which rewards over-smoothing) but maximum recovery of the true genotype states, with coherence a downstream consequence. This is exactly why the caller comparison is read at each caller’s calibrated (minimum-FDR) cutoff rather than at maximal smoothness.
The framework is falsifiable: a caller that produced a smoother, more autocorrelated GWAS profile without a lower ancestry-reconstruction error would support H1 (model-imposed filtering), not H2 (recovery).

§1 missing-data summaries (data/missing_data/, gitignored)
Inputs for the §1 foundations notebooks analysis/missing-data-floor-model.qmd (part 2) and analysis/missing-data-model-comparison.qmd (part 3). Each is a tiny per-sample table of coverage (lambda) and observed missingness (missing_obs):

wgs_per_sample.tsv (1437 samples) — from picard CollectWgsMetrics
wideseq_per_sample.tsv (1434 samples) — 27 M teosinte-variant binned counts
snp50k_per_sample.tsv (1439 samples) — 50 K panel per-site allelic counts

Source tables (native HPC paths, /rsstu — the mount /Volumes/rsstu/... is the same data but unstable):

/rsstu/users/r/rrellan/BZea/bzeaseq/WGSmetrics_summary.tsv (296 KB)
/rsstu/users/r/rrellan/BZea/bzeaseq/ancestry/all_samples_bin_genotypes.tsv (309 MB)
/rsstu/users/r/rrellan/BZea/bzeaseq/50K/allelic_counts50K.tsv (2.4 GB)

How they were built (NCSU Hazel, LSF). The heavy reduction (esp. the 2.4 GB 50K read) runs on a compute node; only tiny summaries are pulled back. Scheduler is LSF.

Code (tracked): scripts/make_missing_data_summaries.R (reduces each source to a per-sample summary with data.table::fread, reading only the needed columns) and its submit wrapper scripts/make_missing_data_summaries.lsf.
R environment: the full bzeaseq.yml conda spec is no longer dependency-solvable, so a minimal env (r-base + r-data.table) is built on the login node (compute nodes have no internet) in fast scratch (ephemeral, ~1 month) by scripts/build_missing_data_env.sh:
bash scripts/build_missing_data_env.sh   # -> $RDT_ENV (default /share/<grp>/<user>/conda/env/rdt)
Submit the reduction (native /rsstu reads, priority queue sara):
cd /share/maize/frodrig4/zealhmm_jobs/missing_data
# (make_missing_data_summaries.{R,lsf} staged here)
bsub < make_missing_data_summaries.lsf     # -> out/{wgs,wideseq,snp50k}_per_sample.tsv
The .lsf activates the rdt env, sets BZEASEQ_DIR=/rsstu/users/r/rrellan/BZea/bzeaseq and OUT_DIR=$PWD/out, and runs the R script (~20 s wall, incl. the 2.4 GB read).
Copy the three summaries back into data/missing_data/ here. They are gitignored (local only); the notebooks read them and the executed render is shipped via the committed docs/ HTML (Pages does not re-execute).

To reproduce anywhere with the mount instead of the HPC, run the same R script with BZEASEQ_DIR=/Volumes/rsstu/users/r/rrellan/BZea/bzeaseq.

Shareable release bundle (release/, gitignored → CyVerse)
scripts/zeal_export_release.R assembles the citable BZea genotype release into release/bzea_genotypes/ (bulk, gitignored; destined for the CyVerse Data Store with a DOI — Phase E of SHAREABLE_DATASET_PLAN.md). Fully reproducible from the tracked script + scripts/release_README.md template; ~156 MB, 30 files.
All five objects share one common callable panel (intersection of every object’s callable lines ≈ 1,395; the 8 near-empty libraries RTIGER’s per-chromosome QC can’t call are dropped) and ship in three encodings: PLINK .bed/.bim/.fam (canonical binary 012), tidy _012.tsv.gz, and the native .rds. Four are ancestry mosaics (rtiger/nnil/binhmm/lbimpute); the fifth, hwe_post_gt, is the genotype — the real bcftools mpileup | call -mv (HWE-prior MAP) calls from bzea_50K_cohort.vcf.gz, extracted for the panel (values = the bcftools calls, not a reconstruction). No single-sample GL genotypes are shared. Shared markers/snp50k_markers.tsv + lines/snp50k_lines.tsv; MANIFEST.tsv carries sha256 per file. The legacy 250K RTIGER introgression set is not in the release (regenerate with recalibrated RTIGER if ever needed).
The cohort VCF is staged from /Volumes/rsstu/.../bzeaseq/50K/results/joint/bzea_50K_cohort.vcf.gz into data/zeal/ (gitignored) and consumed by scripts/zeal_hwe_post_gt.R. The README states the wall (see TERMINOLOGY.md): a _mosaic PLINK/012 file encodes ancestry dosage on the SNP’s ref/alt alleles for tooling compatibility — it is not a genotype and does not report the true allele at invariant sites; only hwe_post does.
